text.title: Match Real World Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every match-real-world application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every UI string flagged MUST cite evidence from the user-vocabulary corpus (ticket id, transcript line, search-log entry) before a substitution is proposed. Substitutions without citation are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Substitutions without citation are team preference, not user vocabulary; documented citations make the audit reviewable and reversible.

rule.statement: When a substitution is applied, it MUST be applied consistently across all surfaces (web + mobile + email + docs). Same concept, same term — inconsistency is itself a heuristic violation.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent terms ("Sign in" / "Login" / "Log in") force users to question whether the actions are the same, breaking the mental model.

rule.statement: Follow established domain conventions (cart, inbox, trash) even when the underlying metaphor is dated. Replace only when user research shows the convention itself confuses the target users.
rule.rationale: Conventions are pre-trained patterns; breaking them costs comprehension. The exception is research-driven, not preference-driven.

rule.statement: Dates, numbers, and addresses MUST follow the user's locale conventions. Ambiguous formats (03/04/2025) MUST be replaced with unambiguous forms (4 Mar 2025 or 2025-03-04).
rule.rationale: Locale-blind formats cause real errors (medical, financial, travel). The unambiguous form is mandatory.

rule.statement: Primary navigation icons MUST be paired with text labels. Icon-only is allowed only for universally-recognised exceptions (magnifying-glass search, X close) with confirmed recognition evidence for the target user population.
rule.rationale: "Universal" icons are not universal; research-confirmed exceptions only.

rule.statement: For domain-expert products, "plain language" means the domain's language. Over-simplifying expert vocabulary (rewriting "invoice" → "bill" in an accounting tool) is a heuristic violation, not an improvement.
rule.rationale: Expert users have precise vocabulary expectations; simplifying removes signal and loses trust.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
